essay_text:
Passover is always a crowded holiday at our house. The day before our week-long celebration of freedom begins,
our kitchen is filled with three generations of women, blasting 80's hits, and the smell of onions. My mom always
reminds me to wait before putting trays hot from the oven onto the counter because “remember when I
accidentally made the glass tray filled with farfel explode?”. I laugh and form sticky dough into kneidlach for the
chicken soup.
For as long as I can remember, I’ve helped prepare this exact meal: the charoset, the farfel, and the kneidlach.
Until I was tall enough, I stood on a step stool, carefully following the recipes that my great-great-grandparents, in
their East European shtetls, passed down to me, in my family’s São Paulo apartment. I read the family
cookbook’s instructions over and over, making sure that I was doing everything exactly right. I measured out
ingredients, chopped vegetables, set timers for the oven slowly and attentively. I felt it was my duty to carry out
these tasks, precisely, identically. But with time, this routine became repetitive, mechanical, mundane. Those old
songs rang in my ears; I sighed as I cut up yet another carrot; I dragged my feet to set up the table. I started to tire
of doing the same things over and over, year after year.
Naturally, however, as the years passed, I realized: Repetition is not an exercise in vain. Repetition yields small
but meaningful growth, personal touches become traditions in and of themselves.
Every year, I'd added my own touches to the holiday proceedings. I’d incorporated customary ingredients—leeks,
potatoes, and bitter herbs—into new dishes. I’d changed the positions of the wine glasses to prevent spilling.
These were simple changes, but, slowly, they became important parts of our seder. I followed the same rules and
traditions, but the dishes grew more complex, and the tablecloth, thankfully, cleaner.
Later on, it’s time for seder. We gather around the plastic tables set up in our jam-packed living room and begin
telling the story of our people. Most of the seder is scripted, but we recount it with fervor as if the story were new to
tell and hear every year. In some ways, it is. Though we sit through hours and hours of storytelling and praying, we
always find new ways to build on our traditions. We turn old Hebrew melodies into Brazilian funk songs. We
scatter the table with masks, stickers, and drawings that represent different parts of the narrative of Passover. We
allow our experiences from each year to expand the way we look at the story already heard so many times.
This is what really makes Passover and tradition so special. Each year, we create new meanings, memories, and
ideas. We retell the same stories—stories that are repeated across generations and across the world—only to find
there is more to uncover.
It is from repetition that novelty emerges. Acknowledging the past’s connection to the present opens us up to
possibility, innovation, and insight. Through preparing the same meals, I came up with new recipes. Through
telling the same stories, we found new angles of interpretation. And I can see this, now, in everything else that
might have otherwise become tiresome. I repeat the same knit and purl stitches to make a new sweater. I replicate
the same procedures in the lab daily to get valid results regarding plant growth. I play the same scales with
students in my music program until they have dominance over the guitar. Yes, repetition can, at times, seem
pointless and annoying, but when I think of sitting with the same people, around the same table, and to the sound
of the same songs on Passover, it fills me with excitement for the new things that come out of our seder every
year.
Community Disruption
8
Schools in Sao Paulo were mandatorily shut down in March, when I was sitting mock exams for the IB. These
were canceled thereafter, as was the IB. When presented with the choice of proceeding with the IB May session,
for which I was signed up, or transferring to the November session, I chose the latter, as I wanted to be able to
take the exams.
My school adhered to online classes during a period when I was taking on two new curricula in addition to the IB.
prompt: Some students have a background, identity, interest, or talent that is so meaningful they believe their application would be incomplete without it. If this sounds like you, then please share your story.
persona_prompt: You are a writer who crafts reflective narratives, weaving personal anecdotes and sensory details with thoughtful, often abstract, conclusions. You value clarity and a gentle, inviting tone, often structuring your arguments by presenting an initial perspective, then revealing a deeper, more nuanced understanding. Your prose is precise yet warm, demonstrating a quiet confidence in your insights.
sentence_style: Varied, transitioning from shorter, descriptive sentences that establish a scene to more complex, reflective sentences that develop a central idea. Sentences flow smoothly, often building on one another, with occasional use of parallel structures for emphasis.
vocabulary_level: Moderate — uses accessible language but integrates precise words like "shtetls," "mundane," "fervor," and "novelty" without being overly academic, often for sensory impact or to convey a nuanced thought.
structural_patterns: The writer employs an anecdotal opening with rich sensory details, followed by a personal narrative arc that reveals an evolution of perspective (from initial boredom to profound appreciation). The essay uses a circular structure, starting and ending with the central theme, while broadening its application to diverse contexts in the middle sections.
distinctive_moves: Sensory Immersion: Creates vivid scenes through rich descriptions of sights, smells, and sounds.
Personal Revelation as Narrative Arc: Traces a journey of understanding, moving from an initial, simpler view to a more mature, nuanced insight.
Connecting Micro to Macro: Links specific family traditions and personal experiences to broader philosophical ideas about growth, innovation, and meaning.
Thesis-driven Reflection: Clearly articulates a central insight or 'realization' and then elaborates on it with illustrative examples.
Use of Direct Dialogue: Incorporates brief, authentic quotes to add warmth and character to the narrative.
themes_and_preoccupations: The evolving nature and profound value of tradition and cultural heritage.
The transformative power of repetition as a catalyst for growth, novelty, and deeper meaning.
The interconnectedness of past experiences, present actions, and future possibilities.
Personal growth achieved through observation, reflection, and engagement with routine.
self_presentation: Thoughtful, observant, humble yet confident in their insights, appreciative of heritage, and intellectually curious. The writer presents themselves as someone who learns and grows significantly from their lived experiences.
cultural_or_contextual_markers: Jewish heritage (Passover, Seder, specific food items like farfel and kneidlach, historical reference to shtetls) and Brazilian cultural influence (São Paulo apartment, Brazilian funk songs), providing a specific, rich, and globalized backdrop.
avoid_in_imitation: Overly casual slang or jargon that would break the reflective tone.
Abrupt transitions between ideas without clear thematic connections.
Purely descriptive passages that lack a deeper, underlying reflection or insight.
A narrative that doesn't demonstrate a clear personal evolution or 'aha!' moment.
sample_sentence: The familiar rhythm of our annual garden planting, a ritual passed down through generations, has slowly revealed itself as a canvas for new seeds of innovation and unexpected beauty.
